revision.comment: Grundinfos
revision.text:
{{Infobox Hieroglyphen |TITEL = Zwölfte Tagesstunde |NAME = M16-G1-p-D45:r-t:N5 |NAME-TRANSKRIPTION = Hapu-seret
''{{Unicode|Ḥ3pw-srt}}''
''Die den [[Re (Ägyptische Mythologie)|Glänzenden]] verbirgt'' }} Die '''zwölfte Tagesstunde''' (auch '''zwölfte Stunde des Tages''') bezeichnete im [[Altes Ägypten|Alten Ägypten]] als zwölfte Stunde des [[Tag (Altes Ägypten)|Tages]] die „Stunde des [[Sonnenuntergang]]s“, die mit dem Beginn der [[Dämmerung|Abenddämmerung]] ([[Erste Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|erste Nachtstunde]]) endete. Die Tageszeiten wurden im Alten Ägypten durch die [[Stundengöttinnen]] repräsentiert. Die zwölfte Tagesstunde trug den Namen „Die den Glänzenden (Re) verbirgt” und kommt unter anderem im „[[Buch vom Tage]]“ vor. == Siehe auch == * [[Erste Tagesstunde (Altes Ägypten)|Erste Stunde des Tages]] == Literatur == * [[Christian Leitz]]: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 9-0683-1669-9 * [[Alexandra von Lieven]]: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch''. The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies (u. a.), Kopenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5 [[Kategorie:Ägyptischer Kalender (Altertum)]] [[Kategorie:Astronomie im Alten Ägypten]] [[Kategorie:Zeitmessung]]

{{Infobox Hieroglyphen |TITEL = Zwölfte Tagesstunde |NAME = M16-G1-p-D45:r-t:N5 |NAME-TRANSKRIPTION = Hapu-seret
''{{Unicode|Ḥ3pw-srt}}''
''Die den [[Re (Ägyptische Mythologie)|Glänzenden]] verbirgt'' }} Die '''zwölfte Tagesstunde''' (auch '''zwölfte Stunde des Tages''') bezeichnete im [[Altes Ägypten|Alten Ägypten]] als zwölfte Stunde des [[Tag (Altes Ägypten)|Tages]] die „Stunde des [[Sonnenuntergang]]s“, die mit dem Beginn der [[Dämmerung|Abenddämmerung]] ([[Erste Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|erste Nachtstunde]]) endete. Die Tageszeiten wurden im Alten Ägypten durch die [[Stundengöttinnen]] repräsentiert. Die zwölfte Tagesstunde trug den Namen „Die den Glänzenden (Re) verbirgt” und kommt unter anderem im „[[Buch vom Tage]]“ vor. Den Erklärungen bezüglich des [[Amduat]]s ist zu entnehmen, dass sich [[Re (Ägyptische Mythologie)|Re]] am Ende der zwölften Tagesstunde in der Region der [[Mesqet]] unmittelbar vor dem „Torweg zur [[Duat]]“ befindet. == Siehe auch == * [[Erste Tagesstunde (Altes Ägypten)|Erste Stunde des Tages]] == Literatur == * [[Christian Leitz]]: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 9-0683-1669-9 * [[Alexandra von Lieven]]: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch''. The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies (u. a.), Kopenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5 [[Kategorie:Ägyptischer Kalender (Altertum)]] [[Kategorie:Astronomie im Alten Ägypten]] [[Kategorie:Zeitmessung]]

{{Infobox Hieroglyphen |TITEL = Zwölfte Tagesstunde |NAME = M16-G1-p-D45:r-t:N5 |NAME-TRANSKRIPTION = Hapu-seret
''{{Unicode|Ḥ3pw-srt}}''
''Die den [[Re (Ägyptische Mythologie)|Glänzenden]] verbirgt'' }} Die '''zwölfte Tagesstunde''' (auch '''zwölfte Stunde des Tages''') bezeichnete im [[Altes Ägypten|Alten Ägypten]] als zwölfte Stunde des [[Tag (Altes Ägypten)|Tages]] die „Stunde des [[Sonnenuntergang]]s“, die mit dem Beginn der [[Dämmerung|Abenddämmerung]] ([[Erste Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|erste Nachtstunde]]) endete. Die Tageszeiten wurden im Alten Ägypten durch die [[Stundengöttinnen]] repräsentiert. Die zwölfte Tagesstunde trug den Namen „Die den Glänzenden (Re) verbirgt” und kommt unter anderem im „[[Buch vom Tage]]“ vor. Den Erklärungen bezüglich des [[Amduat]]s ist zu entnehmen, dass sich [[Re (Ägyptische Mythologie)|Re]] am Ende der zwölften Tagesstunde mit Beginn des Absinkens unter den Horizont in der Region der [[Mesqet]] unmittelbar vor dem „Torweg zur [[Duat]]“ befindet. == Siehe auch == * [[Erste Tagesstunde (Altes Ägypten)|Erste Stunde des Tages]] == Literatur == * [[Christian Leitz]]: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 9-0683-1669-9 * [[Alexandra von Lieven]]: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch''. The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies (u. a.), Kopenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5 [[Kategorie:Ägyptischer Kalender (Altertum)]] [[Kategorie:Astronomie im Alten Ägypten]] [[Kategorie:Zeitmessung]]

{{Infobox Hieroglyphen |TITEL = Zwölfte Tagesstunde |NAME = M16-G1-p-D45:r-t:N5 |NAME-TRANSKRIPTION = Hapu-seret
''{{Unicode|Ḥ3pw-srt}}''
''Die den [[Re (Ägyptische Mythologie)|Glänzenden]] verbirgt'' }} Die '''zwölfte Tagesstunde''' (auch '''zwölfte Stunde des Tages, „Untergehen des Re im Westland“''') bezeichnete im [[Altes Ägypten|Alten Ägypten]] als zwölfte Stunde des [[Tag (Altes Ägypten)|Tages]] die „Stunde des [[Sonnenuntergang]]s“, die mit dem Beginn der [[Dämmerung|Abenddämmerung]] ([[Erste Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|erste Nachtstunde]]) endete. Die Tageszeiten wurden im Alten Ägypten durch die [[Stundengöttinnen]] repräsentiert. Die zwölfte Tagesstunde trug den Namen „Die den Glänzenden (Re) verbirgt” und kommt unter anderem im „[[Buch vom Tage]]“ mit der Benennung „''Untergehen dieses Gottes (Re) im Westland''“ vor. Den Erklärungen bezüglich des [[Amduat]]s ist zu entnehmen, dass sich [[Re (Ägyptische Mythologie)|Re]] am Ende der zwölften Tagesstunde mit Beginn des Absinkens unter den Horizont in der Region der [[Mesqet]] unmittelbar vor dem „Torweg zur [[Duat]]“ befindet. == Siehe auch == * [[Erste Tagesstunde (Altes Ägypten)|Erste Stunde des Tages]] == Literatur == * [[Christian Leitz]]: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 9-0683-1669-9 * [[Alexandra von Lieven]]: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch''. The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies (u. a.), Kopenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5 [[Kategorie:Ägyptischer Kalender (Altertum)]] [[Kategorie:Astronomie im Alten Ägypten]] [[Kategorie:Zeitmessung]]

{{Infobox hieroglyphs|TITEL=Zwölfte Tagesstunde|name=M16-G1-p-D45:r-t:N5|NAME-TRANSKRIPTION=Hapu-seret
''Ḥ3pw-srt''
''Die den [[Re (Ägyptische Mythologie)|Glänzenden]] verbirgt''}}In [[Ancient Egypt|ancient Egypt,]] the '''{{ill|twelfth Hour (Ancient Egypt)|lt=12th Hour|de|Zwölfte Tagesstunde (Altes Ägypten}}''' of the day (also '''twelfth hour of the day, "setting of Re in the west"''' ) was the twelfth hour of the day and was the "hour of [[sunset]] ", which ended with the beginning of [[Twilight|evening twilight]] ( {{Ill|First hour of the night (Ancient Egypt)|lt=First night hour|de|Erste Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|simple|User:Immanuelle/First hour of the night (Ancient Egypt)|en}} ). In ancient Egypt, the times of day were represented by the {{ill|Hour deities|lt=hour goddesses|de|Stundengottheiten}} . The twelfth hour of the day was called “That which hides the shining one (Re)” and appears, among other places, in the “ {{ill|Book of the Day|de|Buch des Tages|en}} ” with the title “ ''The setting of this god (Re) in the Westland'' ”. From the explanations regarding the {{Ill|Amduat|4=de|6=en}}, it can be seen that at the end of the twelfth hour of the day, when [[Ra|Re]] begins to sink below the horizon, he is in the region of the {{Ill|Qenqenet|4=de|6=en}}, immediately in front of the “gateway to the [[Duat]] ”. == See also == * {{ill|First Hour (Ancient Egypt)|lt=1st Hour|de|Erste Tagesstunde (Altes Ägypten)|simple|User:Immanuelle/First Hour (Ancient Egypt)}} == literature == * {{Ill|Christian Leitz|4=de}} : ''Ancient Egyptian star clocks'' . Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 9-0683-1669-9 . * Jürgen Osing: ''Hieratic papyri from Tebtunis I'' . Museum Tusculanum Press, Copenhagen 1998, ISBN 8-7728-9280-3 . * {{Ill|Alexandra von Lieven|4=de}} : ''Outline of the course of the stars – The so-called Nutbook'' . The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies (u. a.), Copenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5 .

From the explanations regarding the {{Ill|Amduat|4=de|6=en}}, it can be seen that at the end of the twelfth hour of the day, when [[Ra|Re]] begins to sink below the horizon, he is in the region of the {{Ill|Qenqenet|4=de|6=en}}, immediately in front of the “gateway to the [[Duat]] ”. == See also == * {{ill|First Hour (Ancient Egypt)|lt=1st Hour|de|Erste Tagesstunde (Altes Ägypten)|simple|User:Immanuelle/First Hour (Ancient Egypt)}} * {{ill|Book of the Day|de|Buch des Tages|en|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Book of the Day}} [[en:User:{{PAGENAME}}]] == literature == * {{Ill|Christian Leitz|4=de}} : ''Ancient Egyptian star clocks'' . Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 9-0683-1669-9 . * Jürgen Osing: ''Hieratic papyri from Tebtunis I'' . Museum Tusculanum Press, Copenhagen 1998, ISBN 8-7728-9280-3 . * {{Ill|Alexandra von Lieven|4=de}} : ''Outline of the course of the stars – The so-called Nutbook'' . The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies (u. a.), Copenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5 . {{draft categories|[[Category:History of timekeeping]] [[Category:Draft articles]] [[Category:Wikipedia drafts]][[en:{{PAGENAME}}]] [[de:Zwölfte Tagesstunde (Altes Ägypten)]] [[fr:User:{{PAGENAME}}]]}}

From the explanations regarding the {{Ill|Amduat|4=de|6=en}}, it can be seen that at the end of the twelfth hour of the day, when [[Ra|Re]] begins to sink below the horizon, he is in the region of the {{Ill|Qenqenet|4=de|6=pt}}, immediately in front of the “gateway to the [[Duat]] ”. == See also == * {{ill|First Hour (Ancient Egypt)|lt=1st Hour|de|Erste Tagesstunde (Altes Ägypten)|simple|User:Immanuelle/First Hour (Ancient Egypt)}} * {{ill|Book of the Day|de|Buch des Tages|en|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Book of the Day}} [[en:User:{{PAGENAME}}]] == literature == * {{Ill|Christian Leitz|4=de}} : ''Ancient Egyptian star clocks'' . Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 9-0683-1669-9 . * Jürgen Osing: ''Hieratic papyri from Tebtunis I'' . Museum Tusculanum Press, Copenhagen 1998, ISBN 8-7728-9280-3 . * {{Ill|Alexandra von Lieven|4=de}} : ''Outline of the course of the stars – The so-called Nutbook'' . The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies (u. a.), Copenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5 . {{draft categories|[[Category:History of timekeeping]] [[Category:Draft articles]] [[Category:Wikipedia drafts]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[simple:User:Immanuelle/Twelfth hour of the day (Ancient Egypt)]][[Category:Ancient Egyptian Day]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[Category:Https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/]] [[en:{{PAGENAME}}]] [[de:Zwölfte Tagesstunde (Altes Ägypten)]] [[fr:User:{{PAGENAME}}]]}}

''That hides the [[Ra|shining one]]''}}{{complex}} In [[Ancient Egypt|ancient Egypt,]] the '''{{ill|Twelfth hour of the day (Ancient Egypt)|lt=12th Hour|de|Zwölfte Tagesstunde (Altes Ägypten|simple|User:Immanuelle/Twelfth hour of the day (Ancient Egypt) }}''' of the day (also '''twelfth hour of the day, "setting of Re in the west"''' ) was the twelfth hour of the day and was the "hour of [[sunset]] ", which ended with the beginning of [[Twilight|evening twilight]] ( {{Ill|First hour of the night (Ancient Egypt)|lt=First night hour|de|Erste Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|simple|User:Immanuelle/First hour of the night (Ancient Egypt)|en}} ). In ancient Egypt, the times of day were represented by the {{ill|Hour deities|lt=hour goddesses|de|Stundengottheiten}} . The twelfth hour of the day was called “That which hides the shining one (Re)” and appears, among other places, in the “ {{ill|Book of the Day|de|Buch des Tages|en|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Book of the Day}} ” with the title “ ''The setting of this god (Re) in the Westland'' ”. From the explanations regarding the {{Ill|Amduat|4=de|6=en}}, it can be seen that at the end of the twelfth hour of the day, when [[Ra|Re]] begins to sink below the horizon, he is in the region of the {{Ill|Qenqenet|4=de|6=pt}}, immediately in front of the “gateway to the [[Duat]] ”. == See also == * {{ill|First Hour (Ancient Egypt)|lt=1st Hour|de|Erste Tagesstunde (Altes Ägypten)|simple|User:Immanuelle/First Hour (Ancient Egypt)}} * {{ill|Book of the Day|de|Buch des Tages|en|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Book of the Day}} [[en:User:{{PAGENAME}}]] == literature == * {{Ill|Christian Leitz|4=de}} : ''Ancient Egyptian star clocks'' . Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 9-0683-1669-9 . * Jürgen Osing: ''Hieratic papyri from Tebtunis I'' . Museum Tusculanum Press, Copenhagen 1998, ISBN 8-7728-9280-3 . * {{Ill|Alexandra von Lieven|4=de}} : ''Outline of the course of the stars – The so-called Nutbook'' . The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies (u. a.), Copenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5 . {{draft categories|[[Category:History of timekeeping]] [[Category:Draft articles]] [[Category:Wikipedia drafts]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[simple:User:Immanuelle/Twelfth hour of the day (Ancient Egypt)]][[Category:Ancient Egyptian Day]] [[Category:new pages]][[en:draft:Twelfth hour of the day (Ancient Egypt)]] [[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[Category:Ancient Egyptian Day]][[Category:new pages]] [[Category:bump 1-25]] [[en:{{PAGENAME}}]] [[de:Zwölfte Tagesstunde (Altes Ägypten)]] [[fr:User:{{PAGENAME}}]]}}

''That hides the [[Ra|shining one]]''}}{{complex}} In [[Ancient Egypt|ancient Egypt,]] the '''{{ill|Twelfth hour of the day (Ancient Egypt)|lt=12th Hour|de|Zwölfte Tagesstunde (Altes Ägypten|simple|User:Immanuelle/Twelfth hour of the day (Ancient Egypt) }}''' of the day (also '''twelfth hour of the day, "setting of Re in the west"''' ) was the twelfth hour of the day and was the "hour of [[sunset]] ", which ended with the beginning of [[Twilight|evening twilight]] ( {{Ill|First hour of the night (Ancient Egypt)|lt=First night hour|de|Erste Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|simple|User:Immanuelle/First hour of the night (Ancient Egypt)|en}} ). In ancient Egypt, the times of day were represented by the {{ill|Hour deities|lt=hour goddesses|de|Stundengottheiten}} . The twelfth hour of the day was called “That which hides the shining one (Re)” and appears, among other places, in the “ {{ill|Book of the Day|de|Buch des Tages|en|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Book of the Day}} ” with the title “ ''The setting of this god (Re) in the Westland'' ”. From the explanations regarding the {{Ill|Amduat|4=de|6=en}}, it can be seen that at the end of the twelfth hour of the day, when [[Ra|Re]] begins to sink below the horizon, he is in the region of the {{Ill|Qenqenet|4=de|6=pt}}, immediately in front of the “gateway to the [[Duat]] ”. == See also == * {{ill|First Hour (Ancient Egypt)|lt=1st Hour|de|Erste Tagesstunde (Altes Ägypten)|simple|User:Immanuelle/First Hour (Ancient Egypt)}} * {{ill|Book of the Day|de|Buch des Tages|en|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Book of the Day}} [[en:User:{{PAGENAME}}]] == literature == * {{IllChristian Leitz|fr|Christian Leitz|en|Christian Leitz|4=de}} : ''Ancient Egyptian star clocks'' . Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 9-0683-1669-9 . * Jürgen Osing: ''Hieratic papyri from Tebtunis I'' . Museum Tusculanum Press, Copenhagen 1998, ISBN 8-7728-9280-3 . * {{Ill|Alexandra von Lieven|fr|Alexandra von Lieven|4=de}} : ''Outline of the course of the stars – The so-called Nutbook'' . In ancient Egypt, the times of day were represented by the {{ill|Hour deities|lt=hour goddesses|de|Stundengottheiten}} . The twelfth hour of the day was called “That which hides the shining one (Re)” and appears, among other places, in the “ {{ill|Book of the Day|de|Buch des Tages|en|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Book of the Day}} ” with the title “ ''The setting of this god (Re) in the Westland'' ”. From the explanations regarding the {{Ill|Amduat|4=de|6=en}}, it can be seen that at the end of the twelfth hour of the day, when [[Ra|Re]] begins to sink below the horizon, he is in the region of the {{Ill|Qenqenet|4=de|6=pt}}, immediately in front of the “gateway to the [[Duat]] ”. == See also == * {{ill|First Hour (Ancient Egypt)|lt=1st Hour|de|Erste Tagesstunde (Altes Ägypten)|simple|User:Immanuelle/First Hour (Ancient Egypt)}} * {{ill|Book of the Day|de|Buch des Tages|en|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Book of the Day}} [[en:User:{{PAGENAME}}]] == literature == * {{Ill|Christian Leitz|fr|Christian Leitz|en|Christian Leitz|4=de}} : ''Ancient Egyptian star clocks'' . {{Infobox hieroglyphs|TITEL=Twelfth hour of the day|name=M16-G1-p-D45:r-t:N5|NAME-TRANSKRIPTION=Hapu-seret
''Ḥ3pw-srt''
''That hides the [[Ra|shining one]]''}}{{complex}} In [[Ancient Egypt|ancient Egypt,]] the '''{{ill|Twelfth hour of the day (Ancient Egypt)|lt=12th Hour|de|Zwölfte Tagesstunde (Altes Ägypten|simple|User:Immanuelle/Twelfth hour of the day (Ancient Egypt) }}''' of the day (also '''twelfth hour of the day, "setting of Re in the west"''' ) was the twelfth hour of the day and was the "hour of [[sunset]] ", which ended with the beginning of [[Twilight|evening twilight]] ( {{Ill|First hour of the night (Ancient Egypt)|lt=First night hour|de|Erste Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|simple|User:Immanuelle/First hour of the night (Ancient Egypt)|en}} ). In ancient Egypt, the times of day were represented by the {{ill|Hour deities|lt=hour goddesses|de|Stundengottheiten}} . The twelfth hour of the day was called “That which hides the shining one (Re)” and appears, among other places, in the “ {{ill|Book of the Day|de|Buch des Tages|en|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Book of the Day}} ” with the title “ ''The setting of this god (Re) in the Westland'' ”. From the explanations regarding the {{Ill|Amduat|4=de|6=en}}, it can be seen that at the end of the twelfth hour of the day, when [[Ra|Re]] begins to sink below the horizon, he is in the region of the {{Ill|Qenqenet|4=de|6=pt}}, immediately in front of the “gateway to the [[Duat]] ”. == See also == * {{ill|First Hour (Ancient Egypt)|lt=1st Hour|de|Erste Tagesstunde (Altes Ägypten)|simple|User:Immanuelle/First Hour (Ancient Egypt)}} * {{ill|Book of the Day|de|Buch des Tages|en|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Book of the Day}} [[en:User:{{PAGENAME}}]] == literature == * {{Ill|Christian Leitz|fr|Christian Leitz|en|Christian Leitz|4=de}} : ''Ancient Egyptian star clocks'' . ''That hides the [[Ra|shining one]]''}}{{complex}} In [[Ancient Egypt|ancient Egypt,]] the '''{{ill|Twelfth hour of the day (Ancient Egypt)|lt=12th Hour|de|Zwölfte Tagesstunde (Altes Ägypten|simple|User:Immanuelle/Twelfth hour of the day (Ancient Egypt) }}''' of the day (also '''twelfth hour of the day, "setting of Re in the west"''' ) was the twelfth hour of the day and was the "hour of [[sunset]] ", which ended with the beginning of [[Twilight|evening twilight]] ( {{Ill|First hour of the night (Ancient Egypt)|lt=First night hour|de|Erste Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|simple|User:Immanuelle/First hour of the night (Ancient Egypt)|en}} ). In ancient Egypt, the times of day were represented by the {{ill|Hour deities|lt=hour goddesses|de|Stundengottheiten}} . The twelfth hour of the day was called “That which hides the shining one (Re)” and appears, among other places, in the “ {{ill|Book of the Day|de|Buch des Tages|en|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Book of the Day}} ” with the title “ ''The setting of this god (Re) in the Westland'' ”. From the explanations regarding the {{Ill|Amduat|4=de|6=en}}, it can be seen that at the end of the twelfth hour of the day, when [[Ra|Re]] begins to sink below the horizon, he is in the region of the {{Ill|Qenqenet|4=de|6=pt}}, immediately in front of the “gateway to the [[Duat]] ”. == See also == * {{ill|First Hour (Ancient Egypt)|lt=1st Hour|de|Erste Tagesstunde (Altes Ägypten)|simple|User:Immanuelle/First Hour (Ancient Egypt)}} * {{ill|Book of the Day|de|Buch des Tages|en|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Book of the Day}} [[en:User:{{PAGENAME}}]] == literature == * {{Ill|Christian Leitz|fr|Christian Leitz|en|Christian Leitz|4=de}} : ''Ancient Egyptian star clocks'' . Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 9-0683-1669-9 . * Jürgen Osing: ''Hieratic papyri from Tebtunis I'' . Museum Tusculanum Press, Copenhagen 1998, ISBN 8-7728-9280-3 . * {{Ill|Alexandra von Lieven|fr|Alexandra von Lieven|4=de}} : ''Outline of the course of the stars – The so-called Nutbook'' . The twelfth hour of the day was called “That which hides the shining one (Re)” and appears, among other places, in the “ {{ill|Book of the Day|de|Buch des Tages|en|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Book of the Day}} ” with the title “ ''The setting of this god (Re) in the Westland'' ”. From the explanations regarding the {{Ill|Amduat|4=de|6=en}}, it can be seen that at the end of the twelfth hour of the day, when [[Ra|Re]] begins to sink below the horizon, he is in the region of the {{Ill|Qenqenet|4=de|6=pt}}, immediately in front of the “gateway to the [[Duat]] ”. == See also == * {{ill|First Hour (Ancient Egypt)|lt=1st Hour|de|Erste Tagesstunde (Altes Ägypten)|simple|User:Immanuelle/First Hour (Ancient Egypt)}} * {{ill|Book of the Day|de|Buch des Tages|en|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Book of the Day}} [[en:User:{{PAGENAME}}]] == literature == * {{Ill|Christian Leitz|fr|Christian Leitz|en|Christian Leitz|4=de}} : ''Ancient Egyptian star clocks'' . Museum Tusculanum Press, Copenhagen 1998, ISBN 8-7728-9280-3 . * {{Ill|Alexandra von Lieven|fr|Alexandra von Lieven|4=de}} : ''Outline of the course of the stars – The so-called Nutbook'' . {{Infobox hieroglyphs|TITEL=Twelfth hour of the day|name=M16-G1-p-D45:r-t:N5|NAME-TRANSKRIPTION=Hapu-seret
''Ḥ3pw-srt''
''That hides the [[Ra|shining one]]''}}{{complex}} In [[Ancient Egypt|ancient Egypt,]] the '''{{ill|Twelfth hour of the day (Ancient Egypt)|lt=12th Hour|de|Zwölfte Tagesstunde (Altes Ägypten|simple|User:Immanuelle/Twelfth hour of the day (Ancient Egypt) }}''' of the day (also '''twelfth hour of the day, "setting of Re in the west"''' ) was the twelfth hour of the day and was the "hour of [[sunset]] ", which ended with the beginning of [[Twilight|evening twilight]] ( {{Ill|First hour of the night (Ancient Egypt)|lt=First night hour|de|Erste Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|simple|User:Immanuelle/First hour of the night (Ancient Egypt)|en}} ). In ancient Egypt, the times of day were represented by the {{ill|Hour deities|lt=hour goddesses|de|Stundengottheiten}} . The twelfth hour of the day was called “That which hides the shining one (Re)” and appears, among other places, in the “ {{ill|Book of the Day|de|Buch des Tages|en|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Book of the Day}} ” with the title “ ''The setting of this god (Re) in the Westland'' ”. From the explanations regarding the {{Ill|Amduat|4=de|6=en}}, it can be seen that at the end of the twelfth hour of the day, when [[Ra|Re]] begins to sink below the horizon, he is in the region of the {{Ill|Qenqenet|4=de|6=pt}}, immediately in front of the “gateway to the [[Duat]] ”. == See also == * {{ill|First Hour (Ancient Egypt)|lt=1st Hour|de|Erste Tagesstunde (Altes Ägypten)|simple|User:Immanuelle/First Hour (Ancient Egypt)}} * {{ill|Book of the Day|de|Buch des Tages|en|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Book of the Day}} [[en:User:{{PAGENAME}}]] == literature == * {{Ill|Christian Leitz|fr|Christian Leitz|en|Christian Leitz|4=de}} : ''Ancient Egyptian star clocks'' . Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 9-0683-1669-9 . * Jürgen Osing: ''Hieratic papyri from Tebtunis I'' . Museum Tusculanum Press, Copenhagen 1998, ISBN 8-7728-9280-3 . * {{Ill|Alexandra von Lieven|fr|Alexandra von Lieven|4=de}} : ''Outline of the course of the stars – The so-called Nutbook'' . The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies (u. a.), Copenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5 . {{draft categories|[[Category:History of timekeeping]] [[Category:Draft articles]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:deprecated qq open]]

The twelfth hour of the day was called “That which hides the shining one (Re)” and appears, among other places, in the “ {{ill|Book of the Day|de|Buch des Tages|en|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Book of the Day}} ” with the title “ ''The setting of this god (Re) in the Westland'' ”. From the explanations regarding the {{Ill|Amduat|4=de|6=en}}, it can be seen that at the end of the twelfth hour of the day, when [[Ra|Re]] begins to sink below the horizon, he is in the region of the {{Ill|Qenqenet|4=de|6=pt}}, immediately in front of the “gateway to the [[Duat]] ”. == See also == * {{ill|First Hour (Ancient Egypt)|lt=1st Hour|de|Erste Tagesstunde (Altes Ägypten)|simple|User:Immanuelle/First Hour (Ancient Egypt)}} * {{ill|Book of the Day|de|Buch des Tages|en|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Book of the Day}} [[en:User:{{PAGENAME}}]] == literature == * {{Ill|Christian Leitz|fr|Christian Leitz|en|Christian Leitz|4=de}} : ''Ancient Egyptian star clocks'' . ''That hides the [[Ra|shining one]]''}}{{complex}} In [[Ancient Egypt|ancient Egypt,]] the '''{{ill|Twelfth hour of the day (Ancient Egypt)|lt=12th Hour|de|Zwölfte Tagesstunde (Altes Ägypten|simple|User:Immanuelle/Twelfth hour of the day (Ancient Egypt) }}''' of the day (also '''twelfth hour of the day, "setting of Re in the west"''' ) was the twelfth hour of the day and was the "hour of [[sunset]] ", which ended with the beginning of [[Twilight|evening twilight]] ( {{Ill|First hour of the night (Ancient Egypt)|lt=First night hour|de|Erste Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|simple|User:Immanuelle/First hour of the night (Ancient Egypt)|en}} ). In ancient Egypt, the times of day were represented by the {{ill|Hour deities|lt=hour goddesses|de|Stundengottheiten}} . The twelfth hour of the day was called “That which hides the shining one (Re)” and appears, among other places, in the “ {{ill|Book of the Day|de|Buch des Tages|en|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Book of the Day}} ” with the title “ ''The setting of this god (Re) in the Westland'' ”. ''That hides the [[Ra|shining one]]''}}{{complex}} In [[Ancient Egypt|ancient Egypt,]] the '''{{ill|Twelfth hour of the day (Ancient Egypt)|lt=12th Hour|de|Zwölfte Tagesstunde (Altes Ägypten|simple|User:Immanuelle/Twelfth hour of the day (Ancient Egypt) }}''' of the day (also '''twelfth hour of the day, "setting of Re in the west"''' ) was the twelfth hour of the day and was the "hour of [[sunset]] ", which ended with the beginning of [[Twilight|evening twilight]] ( {{Ill|First hour of the night (Ancient Egypt)|lt=First night hour|de|Erste Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|simple|User:Immanuelle/First hour of the night (Ancient Egypt)|en}} ). In ancient Egypt, the times of day were represented by the {{ill|Hour deities|lt=hour goddesses|de|Stundengottheiten}} . The twelfth hour of the day was called “That which hides the shining one (Re)” and appears, among other places, in the “ {{ill|Book of the Day|de|Buch des Tages|en|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Book of the Day}} ” with the title “ ''The setting of this god (Re) in the Westland'' ”. From the explanations regarding the {{Ill|Amduat|4=de|6=en}}, it can be seen that at the end of the twelfth hour of the day, when [[Ra|Re]] begins to sink below the horizon, he is in the region of the {{Ill|Qenqenet|4=de|6=pt}}, immediately in front of the “gateway to the [[Duat]] ”. == See also == * {{ill|First Hour (Ancient Egypt)|lt=1st Hour|de|Erste Tagesstunde (Altes Ägypten)|simple|User:Immanuelle/First Hour (Ancient Egypt)}} * {{ill|Book of the Day|de|Buch des Tages|en|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Book of the Day}} [[en:User:{{PAGENAME}}]] == literature == * {{Ill|Christian Leitz|fr|Christian Leitz|en|Christian Leitz|4=de}} : ''Ancient Egyptian star clocks'' . Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 9-0683-1669-9 . * Jürgen Osing: ''Hieratic papyri from Tebtunis I'' . Museum Tusculanum Press, Copenhagen 1998, ISBN 8-7728-9280-3 . * {{Ill|Alexandra von Lieven|fr|Alexandra von Lieven|4=de}} : ''Outline of the course of the stars – The so-called Nutbook'' . From the explanations regarding the {{Ill|Amduat|4=de|6=en}}, it can be seen that at the end of the twelfth hour of the day, when [[Ra|Re]] begins to sink below the horizon, he is in the region of the {{Ill|Qenqenet|4=de|6=pt}}, immediately in front of the “gateway to the [[Duat]] ”. == See also == * {{ill|First Hour (Ancient Egypt)|lt=1st Hour|de|Erste Tagesstunde (Altes Ägypten)|simple|User:Immanuelle/First Hour (Ancient Egypt)}} * {{ill|Book of the Day|de|Buch des Tages|en|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Book of the Day}} [[en:User:{{PAGENAME}}]] == literature == * {{Ill|Christian Leitz|fr|Christian Leitz|en|Christian Leitz|4=de}} : ''Ancient Egyptian star clocks'' . Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 9-0683-1669-9 . * Jürgen Osing: ''Hieratic papyri from Tebtunis I'' . Museum Tusculanum Press, Copenhagen 1998, ISBN 8-7728-9280-3 . * {{Ill|Alexandra von Lieven|fr|Alexandra von Lieven|4=de}} : ''Outline of the course of the stars – The so-called Nutbook'' . ''Ḥ3pw-srt''
''That hides the [[Ra|shining one]]''}}{{complex}} In [[Ancient Egypt|ancient Egypt,]] the '''{{ill|Twelfth hour of the day (Ancient Egypt)|lt=12th Hour|de|Zwölfte Tagesstunde (Altes Ägypten|simple|User:Immanuelle/Twelfth hour of the day (Ancient Egypt) }}''' of the day (also '''twelfth hour of the day, "setting of Re in the west"''' ) was the twelfth hour of the day and was the "hour of [[sunset]] ", which ended with the beginning of [[Twilight|evening twilight]] ( {{Ill|First hour of the night (Ancient Egypt)|lt=First night hour|de|Erste Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|simple|User:Immanuelle/First hour of the night (Ancient Egypt)|en}} ). In ancient Egypt, the times of day were represented by the {{ill|Hour deities|lt=hour goddesses|de|Stundengottheiten}} . The twelfth hour of the day was called “That which hides the shining one (Re)” and appears, among other places, in the “ {{ill|Book of the Day|de|Buch des Tages|en|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Book of the Day}} ” with the title “ ''The setting of this god (Re) in the Westland'' ”. From the explanations regarding the {{Ill|Amduat|4=de|6=en}}, it can be seen that at the end of the twelfth hour of the day, when [[Ra|Re]] begins to sink below the horizon, he is in the region of the {{Ill|Qenqenet|4=de|6=pt}}, immediately in front of the “gateway to the [[Duat]] ”. == See also == * {{ill|First Hour (Ancient Egypt)|lt=1st Hour|de|Erste Tagesstunde (Altes Ägypten)|simple|User:Immanuelle/First Hour (Ancient Egypt)}} * {{ill|Book of the Day|de|Buch des Tages|en|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Book of the Day}} [[en:User:{{PAGENAME}}]] == literature == * {{Ill|Christian Leitz|fr|Christian Leitz|en|Christian Leitz|4=de}} : ''Ancient Egyptian star clocks'' . From the explanations regarding the {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Amduat|4=de|6=en}}, it can be seen that at the end of the twelfth hour of the day, when [[Ra|Re]] begins to sink below the horizon, he is in the region of the {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Qenqenet|4=de|6=pt}}, immediately in front of the “gateway to the [[Duat]] ”. == See also == * {{ill|First Hour (Ancient Egypt)|lt=1st Hour|de|Erste Tagesstunde (Altes Ägypten)|simple|User:Immanuelle/First Hour (Ancient Egypt)}} * {{ill|Book of the Day|de|Buch des Tages|en|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Book of the Day}} [[en:User:{{PAGENAME}}]] == literature == * {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Christian Leitz|fr|Christian Leitz|en|Christian Leitz|4=de}} : ''Ancient Egyptian star clocks'' . Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 9-0683-1669-9 . * Jürgen Osing: ''Hieratic papyri from Tebtunis I'' . Museum Tusculanum Press, Copenhagen 1998, ISBN 8-7728-9280-3 . * {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Alexandra von Lieven|fr|Alexandra von Lieven|4=de}} : ''Outline of the course of the stars – The so-called Nutbook'' . ''Ḥ3pw-srt''
''That hides the [[Ra|shining one]]''}}{{complex}} In [[Ancient Egypt|ancient Egypt,]] the '''{{ill|Twelfth hour of the day (Ancient Egypt)|lt=12th Hour|de|Zwölfte Tagesstunde (Altes Ägypten|simple|User:Immanuelle/Twelfth hour of the day (Ancient Egypt) }}''' of the day (also '''twelfth hour of the day, "setting of Re in the west"''' ) was the twelfth hour of the day and was the "hour of [[sunset]] ", which ended with the beginning of [[Twilight|evening twilight]] ( {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|First hour of the night (Ancient Egypt)|lt=First night hour|de|Erste Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|simple|User:Immanuelle/First hour of the night (Ancient Egypt)|en}} ). In ancient Egypt, the times of day were represented by the {{ill|Hour deities|lt=hour goddesses|de|Stundengottheiten}} . The twelfth hour of the day was called “That which hides the shining one (Re)” and appears, among other places, in the “ {{ill|Book of the Day|de|Buch des Tages|en|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Book of the Day}} ” with the title “ ''The setting of this god (Re) in the Westland'' ”. From the explanations regarding the {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Amduat|4=de|6=en}}, it can be seen that at the end of the twelfth hour of the day, when [[Ra|Re]] begins to sink below the horizon, he is in the region of the {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Qenqenet|4=de|6=pt}}, immediately in front of the “gateway to the [[Duat]] ”. == See also == * {{ill|First Hour (Ancient Egypt)|lt=1st Hour|de|Erste Tagesstunde (Altes Ägypten)|simple|User:Immanuelle/First Hour (Ancient Egypt)}} * {{ill|Book of the Day|de|Buch des Tages|en|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Book of the Day}} [[en:User:{{PAGENAME}}]] == literature == * {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Christian Leitz|fr|Christian Leitz|en|Christian Leitz|4=de}} : ''Ancient Egyptian star clocks'' . In ancient Egypt, the times of day were represented by the {{ill|Hour deities|lt=hour goddesses|de|Stundengottheiten}} . The twelfth hour of the day was called “That which hides the shining one (Re)” and appears, among other places, in the “ {{ill|Book of the Day|de|Buch des Tages|en|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Book of the Day}} ” with the title “ ''The setting of this god (Re) in the Westland'' ”. From the explanations regarding the {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Amduat|4=de|6=en}}, it can be seen that at the end of the twelfth hour of the day, when [[Ra|Re]] begins to sink below the horizon, he is in the region of the {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Qenqenet|4=de|6=pt}}, immediately in front of the “gateway to the [[Duat]] ”. == See also == * {{ill|First hour of the day (Ancient Egypt)|lt=1st Hour|de|Erste Tagesstunde (Altes Ägypten)|simple|User:Immanuelle/First hour of the day (Ancient Egypt)}} * {{ill|Book of the Day|de|Buch des Tages|en|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Book of the Day}} [[en:User:{{PAGENAME}}]] == literature == * {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Christian Leitz|fr|Christian Leitz|en|Christian Leitz|4=de}} : ''Ancient Egyptian star clocks'' . In ancient Egypt, the times of day were represented by the {{ill|Hour deities|lt=hour goddesses|de|Stundengottheiten|12=draft}} . The twelfth hour of the day was called “That which hides the shining one (Re)” and appears, among other places, in the “ {{ill|Book of the Day|de|Buch des Tages|en|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Book of the Day|12=draft}} ” with the title “ ''The setting of this god (Re) in the Westland'' ”. From the explanations regarding the {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Amduat|4=de|6=en}}, it can be seen that at the end of the twelfth hour of the day, when [[Ra|Re]] begins to sink below the horizon, he is in the region of the {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Qenqenet|4=de|6=pt}}, immediately in front of the “gateway to the [[Duat]] ”. == See also == * {{ill|First hour of the day (Ancient Egypt)|lt=1st Hour|de|Erste Tagesstunde (Altes Ägypten)|simple|User:Immanuelle/First hour of the day (Ancient Egypt)|12=draft}} * {{ill|Book of the Day|de|Buch des Tages|en|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Book of the Day|12=draft}} == literature == * {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Christian Leitz|fr|Christian Leitz|en|Christian Leitz|4=de}} : ''Ancient Egyptian star clocks'' . Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 9-0683-1669-9 . * Jürgen Osing: ''Hieratic papyri from Tebtunis I'' . Museum Tusculanum Press, Copenhagen 1998, ISBN 8-7728-9280-3 . * {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Alexandra von Lieven|fr|Alexandra von Lieven|4=de}} : ''Outline of the course of the stars – The so-called Nutbook'' . The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies (u. a.), Copenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5 . {{draft categories| [[simple:User:Immanuelle/Twelfth hour of the day (Ancient Egypt)]] }} {{draft categories| [[Category:History of timekeeping]] [[Category:Draft articles]] [[Category:Ancient Egyptian Day]] [[Category:Ancient Egyptian Hours]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:Twelfth hour of the day (Ancient Egypt)]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:pages which might possibly be incomplete but are most likely complete]] [[Category:I think I cleared all the incomplete or blank pages earlier]] }} [[en:User:{{PAGENAME}}]] [[en:draft:Twelfth hour of the day (Ancient Egypt)]] [[sn:Twelfth hour of the day (Ancient Egypt)]] [[en:{{PAGENAME}}]] [[de:Zwölfte Tagesstunde (Altes Ägypten)]] [[fr:User:{{PAGENAME}}]] [[sn:welfth hour of the day (Ancient Egypt)]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Twelfth hour of the day (Ancient Egypt)}}

''Ḥ3pw-srt''
''That hides the [[Ra|shining one]]''}}{{complex}} In [[Ancient Egypt|ancient Egypt,]] the '''{{ill|Twelfth hour of the day (Ancient Egypt)|lt=12th Hour|de|Zwölfte Tagesstunde (Altes Ägypten|simple|User:Immanuelle/Twelfth hour of the day (Ancient Egypt)|12=draft|12=draft}}''' of the day (also '''twelfth hour of the day, "setting of Re in the west"''' ) was the twelfth hour of the day and was the "hour of [[sunset]] ", which ended with the beginning of [[Twilight|evening twilight]] ( {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|First hour of the night (Ancient Egypt)|lt=First night hour|de|Erste Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|simple|User:Immanuelle/First hour of the night (Ancient Egypt)|en}} ). In ancient Egypt, the times of day were represented by the {{ill|Hour deities|lt=hour goddesses|de|Stundengottheiten|12=draft|12=draft}} . The twelfth hour of the day was called “That which hides the shining one (Re)” and appears, among other places, in the “ {{ill|Book of the Day|de|Buch des Tages|en|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Book of the Day|12=draft|12=draft}} ” with the title “ ''The setting of this god (Re) in the Westland'' ”. From the explanations regarding the {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Amduat|4=de|6=en}}, it can be seen that at the end of the twelfth hour of the day, when [[Ra|Re]] begins to sink below the horizon, he is in the region of the {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Qenqenet|4=de|6=pt}}, immediately in front of the “gateway to the [[Duat]] ”. == See also == * {{ill|First hour of the day (Ancient Egypt)|lt=1st Hour|de|Erste Tagesstunde (Altes Ägypten)|simple|User:Immanuelle/First hour of the day (Ancient Egypt)|12=draft|12=draft}} * {{ill|Book of the Day|de|Buch des Tages|en|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Book of the Day|12=draft|12=draft}} == literature == * {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Christian Leitz|fr|Christian Leitz|en|Christian Leitz|4=de}} : ''Ancient Egyptian star clocks'' . Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 9-0683-1669-9 . * Jürgen Osing: ''Hieratic papyri from Tebtunis I'' . Museum Tusculanum Press, Copenhagen 1998, ISBN 8-7728-9280-3 . * {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Alexandra von Lieven|fr|Alexandra von Lieven|4=de}} : ''Outline of the course of the stars – The so-called Nutbook'' . The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies (u. a.), Copenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5 . {{draft categories|[[simple:User:Immanuelle/Twelfth hour of the day (Ancient Egypt)]] }} {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:History of timekeeping]] [[Category:Draft articles]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]] [[Category:Ancient Egyptian Hours]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:Twelfth hour of the day (Ancient Egypt)]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:pages which might possibly be incomplete but are most likely complete]] [[Category:I think I cleared all the incomplete or blank pages earlier]] }} [[en:User:{{PAGENAME}}]] [[en:draft:Twelfth hour of the day (Ancient Egypt)]] [[sn:Twelfth hour of the day (Ancient Egypt)]] [[en:{{PAGENAME}}]] [[de:Zwölfte Tagesstunde (Altes Ägypten)]] [[fr:User:{{PAGENAME}}]] [[sn:welfth hour of the day (Ancient Egypt)]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Twelfth hour of the day (Ancient Egypt)}}

12 revisions imported: Bot: import full history from de:Zwölfte Tagesstunde (Altes Ägypten) up to rev 166733299
revision.text:
{{Infobox Hieroglyphen |TITEL = Zwölfte Tagesstunde |NAME = M16-G1-p-D45:r-t:N5 |NAME-TRANSKRIPTION = Hapu-seret
''Ḥ3pw-srt''
''Die den [[Re (Ägyptische Mythologie)|Glänzenden]] verbirgt'' }} Die '''zwölfte Tagesstunde''' (auch '''zwölfte Stunde des Tages, „Untergehen des Re im Westland“''') bezeichnete im [[Altes Ägypten|Alten Ägypten]] als zwölfte Stunde des [[Tag (Altes Ägypten)|Tages]] die „Stunde des [[Sonnenuntergang]]s“, die mit dem Beginn der [[Dämmerung|Abenddämmerung]] ([[Erste Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|erste Nachtstunde]]) endete. Die Tageszeiten wurden im Alten Ägypten durch die [[Stundengöttinnen]] repräsentiert. Die zwölfte Tagesstunde trug den Namen „Die den Glänzenden (Re) verbirgt“ und kommt unter anderem im „[[Buch vom Tage]]“ mit der Benennung „''Untergehen dieses Gottes (Re) im Westland''“ vor. Den Erklärungen bezüglich des [[Amduat]]s ist zu entnehmen, dass sich [[Re (Ägyptische Mythologie)|Re]] am Ende der zwölften Tagesstunde mit Beginn des Absinkens unter den Horizont in der Region der [[Qenqenet]] unmittelbar vor dem „Torweg zur [[Duat]]“ befindet. == Siehe auch == * [[Erste Tagesstunde (Altes Ägypten)|Erste Stunde des Tages]] == Literatur == * [[Christian Leitz]]: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 9-0683-1669-9. * Jürgen Osing: ''Hieratische Papyri aus Tebtunis I''. Museum Tusculanum Press, Copenhagen 1998, ISBN 8-7728-9280-3. * [[Alexandra von Lieven]]: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch''. The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies (u. a.), Kopenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5. {{SORTIERUNG:Zwolfte Tagesstunde}} [[Kategorie:Ägyptischer Kalender (Altertum)]] [[Kategorie:Astronomie im Alten Ägypten]] [[Kategorie:Geschichte der Zeitmessung]]

revision.comment: Immanuelle moved page [[Zwölfte Tagesstunde (Altes Ägypten)]] to [[Twelfth hour of the day (Ancient Egypt)]] without leaving a redirect: Bot: merge import

{{Infobox Hieroglyphen |TITEL = Zwölfte Tagesstunde |NAME = M16-G1-p-D45:r-t:N5 |NAME-TRANSKRIPTION = Hapu-seret
''Ḥ3pw-srt''
''Die den [[Re (Ägyptische Mythologie)|Glänzenden]] verbirgt'' }} Die '''zwölfte Tagesstunde''' (auch '''zwölfte Stunde des Tages, „Untergehen des Re im Westland“''') bezeichnete im [[Altes Ägypten|Alten Ägypten]] als zwölfte Stunde des [[Tag (Altes Ägypten)|Tages]] die „Stunde des [[Sonnenuntergang]]s“, die mit dem Beginn der [[Dämmerung|Abenddämmerung]] ([[Erste Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|erste Nachtstunde]]) endete. Die Tageszeiten wurden im Alten Ägypten durch die [[Stundengöttinnen]] repräsentiert. Die zwölfte Tagesstunde trug den Namen „Die den Glänzenden (Re) verbirgt“ und kommt unter anderem im „[[Buch vom Tage]]“ mit der Benennung „''Untergehen dieses Gottes (Re) im Westland''“ vor. Den Erklärungen bezüglich des [[Amduat]]s ist zu entnehmen, dass sich [[Re (Ägyptische Mythologie)|Re]] am Ende der zwölften Tagesstunde mit Beginn des Absinkens unter den Horizont in der Region der [[Qenqenet]] unmittelbar vor dem „Torweg zur [[Duat]]“ befindet. == Siehe auch == * [[Erste Tagesstunde (Altes Ägypten)|Erste Stunde des Tages]] == Literatur == * [[Christian Leitz]]: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 9-0683-1669-9. * Jürgen Osing: ''Hieratische Papyri aus Tebtunis I''. Museum Tusculanum Press, Copenhagen 1998, ISBN 8-7728-9280-3. * [[Alexandra von Lieven]]: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch''. The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies (u. a.), Kopenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5. {{SORTIERUNG:Zwolfte Tagesstunde}} [[Kategorie:Ägyptischer Kalender (Altertum)]] [[Kategorie:Astronomie im Alten Ägypten]] [[Kategorie:Geschichte der Zeitmessung]]

revision.text:
{{afc comment|1=enwiki might already cover the hours here in [[Amduat]][[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:47, 25 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox hieroglyphs|TITEL=Twelfth hour of the day|name=M16-G1-p-D45:r-t:N5|NAME-TRANSKRIPTION=Hapu-seret
''Ḥ3pw-srt''
''That hides the [[Ra|shining one]]''}} In [[Ancient Egypt|ancient Egypt,]] the '''{{ill|Twelfth hour of the day (Ancient Egypt)|lt=12th Hour|de|Zwölfte Tagesstunde (Altes Ägypten|simple|User:Immanuelle/Twelfth hour of the day (Ancient Egypt)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Twelfth hour of the day (Ancient Egypt)|qq=}}''' of the day (also '''twelfth hour of the day, "setting of Re in the west"''' ) was the twelfth hour of the day and was the "hour of [[sunset]] ", which ended with the beginning of [[Twilight|evening twilight]] ( {{ill|First hour of the night (Ancient Egypt)|lt=First night hour|de|Erste Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|simple|User:Immanuelle/First hour of the night (Ancient Egypt)|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/First hour of the night (Ancient Egypt)|qq=}} ). In ancient Egypt, the times of day were represented by the {{ill|Hour deities|lt=hour goddesses|de|Stundengottheiten|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hour deities|qq=}} . The twelfth hour of the day was called “That which hides the shining one (Re)” and appears, among other places, in the “ {{ill|Book of the Day|de|Buch des Tages|en|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Book of the Day|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Book of the Day|qq=}} ” with the title “ ''The setting of this god (Re) in the Westland'' ”. From the explanations regarding the {{ill|Amduat|4=de|6=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amduat|qq=}}, it can be seen that at the end of the twelfth hour of the day, when [[Ra|Re]] begins to sink below the horizon, he is in the region of the {{ill|Qenqenet|4=de|6=pt|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Qenqenet|qq=}}, immediately in front of the “gateway to the [[Duat]] ”. == See also == * {{ill|First hour of the day (Ancient Egypt)|lt=1st Hour|de|Erste Tagesstunde (Altes Ägypten)|simple|User:Immanuelle/First hour of the day (Ancient Egypt)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/First hour of the day (Ancient Egypt)|qq=}} * {{ill|Book of the Day|de|Buch des Tages|en|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Book of the Day|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Book of the Day|qq=}} == literature == * {{ill|Christian Leitz|fr|Christian Leitz|en|Christian Leitz|4=de|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Christian Leitz|qq=}} : ''Ancient Egyptian star clocks'' . Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 9-0683-1669-9 . * Jürgen Osing: ''Hieratic papyri from Tebtunis I'' . Museum Tusculanum Press, Copenhagen 1998, ISBN 8-7728-9280-3 . * {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|fr|Alexandra von Lieven|4=de|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Alexandra von Lieven|qq=}} : ''Outline of the course of the stars – The so-called Nutbook'' . The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies (u. a.), Copenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5 . {{translated page|de|Zwölfte Tagesstunde (Altes Ägypten)|version=166733299|comment=Imported full de history}} {{draft categories|[[simple:User:Immanuelle/Twelfth hour of the day (Ancient Egypt)]] }} [[simple:User:Immanuelle/Twelfth hour of the day (Ancient Egypt)]] [[sn:Twelfth hour of the day (Ancient Egypt)]] [[en:User:Twelfth hour of the day (Ancient Egypt)]] [[en:draft:Twelfth hour of the day (Ancient Egypt)]] [[en:Twelfth hour of the day (Ancient Egypt)]] [[de:Zwölfte Tagesstunde (Altes Ägypten)]] [[fr:User:Twelfth hour of the day (Ancient Egypt)]] [[sn:welfth hour of the day (Ancient Egypt)]] [[Category:Astronomie im Alten Ägypten]]

From the explanations regarding the {{ill|Amduat|4=de|6=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amduat|qq=}}, it can be seen that at the end of the twelfth hour of the day, when [[Ra|Re]] begins to sink below the horizon, he is in the region of the {{ill|Qenqenet|4=de|6=pt|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Qenqenet|qq=}}, immediately in front of the “gateway to the [[Duat]] ”. == See also == * {{ill|First hour of the day (Ancient Egypt)|lt=1st Hour|de|Erste Tagesstunde (Altes Ägypten)|simple|User:Immanuelle/First hour of the day (Ancient Egypt)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/First hour of the day (Ancient Egypt)|qq=}} * {{ill|Book of the Day|de|Buch des Tages|en|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Book of the Day|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Book of the Day|qq=}} == literature == * {{ill|Christian Leitz|fr|Christian Leitz|en|Christian Leitz|4=de|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Christian Leitz|qq=}} : ''Ancient Egyptian star clocks'' . Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 9-0683-1669-9 . * Jürgen Osing: ''Hieratic papyri from Tebtunis I'' . Museum Tusculanum Press, Copenhagen 1998, ISBN 8-7728-9280-3 . * {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|fr|Alexandra von Lieven|4=de|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Alexandra von Lieven|qq=}} : ''Outline of the course of the stars – The so-called Nutbook'' . The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies (u. a.), Copenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5 . {{translated page|de|Zwölfte Tagesstunde (Altes Ägypten)|version=166733299|comment=Imported full de history}} {{draft categories|[[simple:User:Immanuelle/Twelfth hour of the day (Ancient Egypt)]] }} [[simple:User:Immanuelle/Twelfth hour of the day (Ancient Egypt)]] [[sn:Twelfth hour of the day (Ancient Egypt)]] [[de:Zwölfte Tagesstunde (Altes Ägypten)]] [[fr:User:Twelfth hour of the day (Ancient Egypt)]] [[sn:welfth hour of the day (Ancient Egypt)]] [[Category:Astronomy in Ancient Egypt]] [[Category:History of timekeeping]] [[Category:Egyptian calendar]]

''Ḥ3pw-srt''
''That hides the [[Ra|shining one]]''}} In [[Ancient Egypt|ancient Egypt,]] the '''{{ill|Twelfth hour of the day (Ancient Egypt)|lt=12th Hour|de|Zwölfte Tagesstunde (Altes Ägypten|simple|User:Immanuelle/Twelfth hour of the day (Ancient Egypt)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Twelfth hour of the day (Ancient Egypt)|qq=}}''' of the day (also '''twelfth hour of the day, "setting of Re in the west"''' ) was the twelfth hour of the day and was the "hour of [[sunset]] ", which ended with the beginning of [[Twilight|evening twilight]] ( {{ill|First hour of the night (Ancient Egypt)|lt=First night hour|de|Erste Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|simple|User:Immanuelle/First hour of the night (Ancient Egypt)|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/First hour of the night (Ancient Egypt)|qq=}} ). In ancient Egypt, the times of day were represented by the {{ill|Hour deities|lt=hour goddesses|de|Stundengottheiten|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hour deities|qq=}} . The twelfth hour of the day was called “That which hides the shining one (Re)” and appears, among other places, in the “ {{ill|Book of the Day|de|Buch des Tages|en|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Book of the Day|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Book of the Day|qq=}} ” with the title “ ''The setting of this god (Re) in the Westland'' ”. From the explanations regarding the {{ill|Amduat|4=de|6=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amduat|qq=}}, it can be seen that at the end of the twelfth hour of the day, when [[Ra|Re]] begins to sink below the horizon, he is in the region of the {{ill|Qenqenet|4=de|6=pt|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Qenqenet|qq=}}, immediately in front of the “gateway to the [[Duat]] ”. == See also == * {{ill|First hour of the day (Ancient Egypt)|lt=1st Hour|de|Erste Tagesstunde (Altes Ägypten)|simple|User:Immanuelle/First hour of the day (Ancient Egypt)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/First hour of the day (Ancient Egypt)|qq=}} * {{ill|Book of the Day|de|Buch des Tages|en|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Book of the Day|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Book of the Day|qq=}} == literature == * {{ill|Christian Leitz|fr|Christian Leitz|en|Christian Leitz|4=de|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Christian Leitz|qq=}} : ''Ancient Egyptian star clocks'' . {{afc comment|1=enwiki might already cover the hours here in [[Amduat]][[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:47, 25 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox hieroglyphs|TITEL=Twelfth hour of the day|name=M16-G1-p-D45:r-t:N5|NAME-TRANSKRIPTION=Hapu-seret
''Ḥ3pw-srt''
''That hides the [[:en:Ra|shining one]]''}} In [[:en:Ancient Egypt|ancient Egypt,]] the '''{{ill|Twelfth hour of the day (Ancient Egypt)|lt=12th Hour|de|Zwölfte Tagesstunde (Altes Ägypten|simple|User:Immanuelle/Twelfth hour of the day (Ancient Egypt)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Twelfth hour of the day (Ancient Egypt)|qq=}}''' of the day (also '''twelfth hour of the day, "setting of Re in the west"''' ) was the twelfth hour of the day and was the "hour of [[:en:Sunset|Sunset]] ", which ended with the beginning of [[:en:Twilight|evening twilight]] ( {{ill|First hour of the night (Ancient Egypt)|lt=First night hour|de|Erste Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|simple|User:Immanuelle/First hour of the night (Ancient Egypt)|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/First hour of the night (Ancient Egypt)|qq=}} ). In ancient Egypt, the times of day were represented by the {{ill|Hour deities|lt=hour goddesses|de|Stundengottheiten|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hour deities|qq=}} . The twelfth hour of the day was called “That which hides the shining one (Re)” and appears, among other places, in the “ {{ill|Book of the Day|de|Buch des Tages|en|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Book of the Day|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Book of the Day|qq=}} ” with the title “ ''The setting of this god (Re) in the Westland'' ”. From the explanations regarding the {{ill|Amduat|4=de|6=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amduat|qq=}}, it can be seen that at the end of the twelfth hour of the day, when [[:en:Ra|Re]] begins to sink below the horizon, he is in the region of the {{ill|Qenqenet|4=de|6=pt|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Qenqenet|qq=}}, immediately in front of the “gateway to the [[:en:Duat|Duat]] ”. == See also == * {{ill|First hour of the day (Ancient Egypt)|lt=1st Hour|de|Erste Tagesstunde (Altes Ägypten)|simple|User:Immanuelle/First hour of the day (Ancient Egypt)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/First hour of the day (Ancient Egypt)|qq=}} * {{ill|Book of the Day|de|Buch des Tages|en|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Book of the Day|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Book of the Day|qq=}} == literature == * {{ill|Christian Leitz|fr|Christian Leitz|en|Christian Leitz|4=de|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Christian Leitz|qq=}} : ''Ancient Egyptian star clocks'' . Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 9-0683-1669-9 . * Jürgen Osing: ''Hieratic papyri from Tebtunis I'' . Museum Tusculanum Press, Copenhagen 1998, ISBN 8-7728-9280-3 . * {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|fr|Alexandra von Lieven|4=de|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Alexandra von Lieven|qq=}} : ''Outline of the course of the stars – The so-called Nutbook'' . The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies (u. a.), Copenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5 . {{translated page|de|Zwölfte Tagesstunde (Altes Ägypten)|version=166733299|comment=Imported full de history}} {{draft categories|[[simple:User:Immanuelle/Twelfth hour of the day (Ancient Egypt)]] }} [[simple:User:Immanuelle/Twelfth hour of the day (Ancient Egypt)]] [[sn:Twelfth hour of the day (Ancient Egypt)]] [[de:Zwölfte Tagesstunde (Altes Ägypten)]] [[fr:User:Twelfth hour of the day (Ancient Egypt)]] [[sn:welfth hour of the day (Ancient Egypt)]] [[Category:History of timekeeping]] [[Category:Egyptian calendar]] [[Category:Ancient Egyptian astronomy]]

''Ḥ3pw-srt''
''That hides the [[:en:Ra|shining one]]''}} In [[:en:Ancient Egypt|ancient Egypt,]] the '''{{ill|Twelfth hour of the day (Ancient Egypt)|lt=12th Hour|de|Zwölfte Tagesstunde (Altes Ägypten|simple|User:Immanuelle/Twelfth hour of the day (Ancient Egypt)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Twelfth hour of the day (Ancient Egypt)|qq=}}''' of the day (also '''twelfth hour of the day, "setting of Re in the west"''' ) was the twelfth hour of the day and was the "hour of [[:en:Sunset|Sunset]] ", which ended with the beginning of [[:en:Twilight|evening twilight]] ( {{ill|First hour of the night (Ancient Egypt)|lt=First night hour|de|Erste Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|simple|User:Immanuelle/First hour of the night (Ancient Egypt)|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/First hour of the night (Ancient Egypt)|qq=}} ). In ancient Egypt, the times of day were represented by the {{ill|Hour deities|lt=hour goddesses|de|Stundengottheiten|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hour deities|qq=}} . The twelfth hour of the day was called “That which hides the shining one (Re)” and appears, among other places, in the “ {{ill|Book of the Day|de|Buch des Tages|en|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Book of the Day|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Book of the Day|qq=}} ” with the title “ ''The setting of this god (Re) in the Westland'' ”. From the explanations regarding the {{ill|Amduat|4=de|6=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amduat|qq=}}, it can be seen that at the end of the twelfth hour of the day, when [[:en:Ra|Re]] begins to sink below the horizon, he is in the region of the {{ill|Qenqenet|4=de|6=pt|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Qenqenet|qq=}}, immediately in front of the “gateway to the [[:en:Duat|Duat]] ”. == See also == * {{ill|First hour of the day (Ancient Egypt)|lt=1st Hour|de|Erste Tagesstunde (Altes Ägypten)|simple|User:Immanuelle/First hour of the day (Ancient Egypt)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/First hour of the day (Ancient Egypt)|qq=}} * {{ill|Book of the Day|de|Buch des Tages|en|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Book of the Day|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Book of the Day|qq=}} == literature == * {{ill|Christian Leitz|fr|Christian Leitz|en|Christian Leitz|4=de|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Christian Leitz|qq=}} : ''Ancient Egyptian star clocks'' . From the explanations regarding the {{ill|Amduat|4=de|6=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amduat|qq=|WD=Q460509}}, it can be seen that at the end of the twelfth hour of the day, when [[:en:Ra|Re]] begins to sink below the horizon, he is in the region of the {{ill|Qenqenet|4=de|6=pt|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Qenqenet|qq=|WD=Q1300614}}, immediately in front of the “gateway to the [[:en:Duat|Duat]] ”. == See also == * {{ill|First hour of the day (Ancient Egypt)|lt=1st Hour|de|Erste Tagesstunde (Altes Ägypten)|simple|User:Immanuelle/First hour of the day (Ancient Egypt)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/First hour of the day (Ancient Egypt)|qq=|WD=Q1362046}} * {{ill|Book of the Day|de|Buch des Tages|en|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Book of the Day|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Book of the Day|qq=|WD=Q997784}} == literature == * {{ill|Christian Leitz|fr|Christian Leitz|en|Christian Leitz|4=de|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Christian Leitz|qq=|WD=Q1080802}} : ''Ancient Egyptian star clocks'' . [[de:Zwölfte Tagesstunde (Altes Ägypten)]] {{afc comment|1=enwiki might already cover the hours here in [[Amduat]][[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:47, 25 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox hieroglyphs|TITEL=Twelfth hour of the day|name=M16-G1-p-D45:r-t:N5|NAME-TRANSKRIPTION=Hapu-seret
''Ḥ3pw-srt''
''That hides the [[:en:Ra|shining one]]''}} In [[:en:Ancient Egypt|ancient Egypt,]] the '''{{ill|Twelfth hour of the day (Ancient Egypt)|lt=12th Hour|de|Zwölfte Tagesstunde (Altes Ägypten|simple|User:Immanuelle/Twelfth hour of the day (Ancient Egypt)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Twelfth hour of the day (Ancient Egypt)|qq=|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work}}''' of the day (also '''twelfth hour of the day, "setting of Re in the west"''' ) was the twelfth hour of the day and was the "hour of [[:en:Sunset|Sunset]] ", which ended with the beginning of [[:en:Twilight|evening twilight]] ( {{ill|First hour of the night (Ancient Egypt)|lt=First night hour|de|Erste Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|simple|User:Immanuelle/First hour of the night (Ancient Egypt)|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/First hour of the night (Ancient Egypt)|qq=|WD=Q1361948}} ). In ancient Egypt, the times of day were represented by the {{ill|Hour deities|lt=hour goddesses|de|Stundengottheiten|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hour deities|qq=|WD=Q2359142}} . The twelfth hour of the day was called “That which hides the shining one (Re)” and appears, among other places, in the “ {{ill|Book of the Day|de|Buch des Tages|en|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Book of the Day|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Book of the Day|qq=|WD=Q997784}} ” with the title “ ''The setting of this god (Re) in the Westland'' ”. From the explanations regarding the {{ill|Amduat|4=de|6=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amduat|qq=|WD=Q460509}}, it can be seen that at the end of the twelfth hour of the day, when [[:en:Ra|Re]] begins to sink below the horizon, he is in the region of the {{ill|Qenqenet|4=de|6=pt|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Qenqenet|qq=|WD=Q1300614}}, immediately in front of the “gateway to the [[:en:Duat|Duat]] ”. == See also == * {{ill|First hour of the day (Ancient Egypt)|lt=1st Hour|de|Erste Tagesstunde (Altes Ägypten)|simple|User:Immanuelle/First hour of the day (Ancient Egypt)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/First hour of the day (Ancient Egypt)|qq=|WD=Q1362046}} * {{ill|Book of the Day|de|Buch des Tages|en|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Book of the Day|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Book of the Day|qq=|WD=Q997784}} == literature == * {{ill|Christian Leitz|fr|Christian Leitz|en|Christian Leitz|4=de|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Christian Leitz|qq=|WD=Q1080802}} : ''Ancient Egyptian star clocks'' . Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 9-0683-1669-9 . * Jürgen Osing: ''Hieratic papyri from Tebtunis I'' . Museum Tusculanum Press, Copenhagen 1998, ISBN 8-7728-9280-3 . * {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|fr|Alexandra von Lieven|4=de|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Alexandra von Lieven|qq=|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work}} : ''Outline of the course of the stars – The so-called Nutbook'' . The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies (u. a.), Copenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5 . {{translated page|de|Zwölfte Tagesstunde (Altes Ägypten)|version=166733299|comment=Imported full de history}} {{draft categories| }} [[de:Zwölfte Tagesstunde (Altes Ägypten)]] [[fr:User:Twelfth hour of the day (Ancient Egypt)]] [[Category:History of timekeeping]] [[Category:Egyptian calendar]] [[Category:Ancient Egyptian astronomy]] {{wikidata link|Q245251}} [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:Wikidata no English label]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:qq]] {{wikidata link|Q245251}} [[Category:qq]][[Category:qq]]

From the explanations regarding the {{ill|Amduat|4=de|6=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amduat|qq=|WD=Q460509}}, it can be seen that at the end of the twelfth hour of the day, when [[:en:Ra|Re]] begins to sink below the horizon, he is in the region of the {{ill|Qenqenet|4=de|6=pt|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Qenqenet|qq=|WD=Q1300614}}, immediately in front of the “gateway to the [[:en:Duat|Duat]] ”. == See also == * {{ill|First hour of the day (Ancient Egypt)|lt=1st Hour|de|Erste Tagesstunde (Altes Ägypten)|simple|User:Immanuelle/First hour of the day (Ancient Egypt)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/First hour of the day (Ancient Egypt)|qq=|WD=Q1362046}} * {{ill|Book of the Day|de|Buch des Tages|en|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Book of the Day|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Book of the Day|qq=|WD=Q997784}} == literature == * {{ill|Christian Leitz|fr|Christian Leitz|en|Christian Leitz|4=de|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Christian Leitz|qq=|WD=Q1080802}} : ''Ancient Egyptian star clocks'' . ''That hides the [[:en:Ra|shining one]]''}} In [[:en:Ancient Egypt|ancient Egypt,]] the '''{{ill|Twelfth hour of the day (Ancient Egypt)|lt=12th Hour|de|Zwölfte Tagesstunde (Altes Ägypten|simple|User:Immanuelle/Twelfth hour of the day (Ancient Egypt)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Twelfth hour of the day (Ancient Egypt)|qq=|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work}}''' of the day (also '''twelfth hour of the day, "setting of Re in the west"''' ) was the twelfth hour of the day and was the "hour of [[:en:Sunset|Sunset]] ", which ended with the beginning of [[:en:Twilight|evening twilight]] ( {{ill|First hour of the night (Ancient Egypt)|lt=First night hour|de|Erste Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|simple|User:Immanuelle/First hour of the night (Ancient Egypt)|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/First hour of the night (Ancient Egypt)|qq=|WD=Q1361948}} ). In ancient Egypt, the times of day were represented by the {{ill|Hour deities|lt=hour goddesses|de|Stundengottheiten|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hour deities|qq=|WD=Q2359142}} . The twelfth hour of the day was called “That which hides the shining one (Re)” and appears, among other places, in the “ {{ill|Book of the Day|de|Buch des Tages|en|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Book of the Day|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Book of the Day|qq=|WD=Q997784}} ” with the title “ ''The setting of this god (Re) in the Westland'' ”. From the explanations regarding the {{ill|Amduat|4=de|6=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amduat|qq=|WD=Q460509}}, it can be seen that at the end of the twelfth hour of the day, when [[:en:Ra|Re]] begins to sink below the horizon, he is in the region of the {{ill|Qenqenet|4=de|6=pt|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Qenqenet|qq=|WD=Q1300614}}, immediately in front of the “gateway to the [[:en:Duat|Duat]] ”. == See also == * {{ill|First hour of the day (Ancient Egypt)|lt=1st Hour|de|Erste Tagesstunde (Altes Ägypten)|simple|User:Immanuelle/First hour of the day (Ancient Egypt)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/First hour of the day (Ancient Egypt)|qq=|WD=Q1362046}} * {{ill|Book of the Day|de|Buch des Tages|en|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Book of the Day|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Book of the Day|qq=|WD=Q997784}} == literature == * {{ill|Christian Leitz|fr|Christian Leitz|en|Christian Leitz|4=de|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Christian Leitz|qq=|WD=Q1080802}} : ''Ancient Egyptian star clocks'' . Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 9-0683-1669-9 . * Jürgen Osing: ''Hieratic papyri from Tebtunis I'' . Museum Tusculanum Press, Copenhagen 1998, ISBN 8-7728-9280-3 . * {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|fr|Alexandra von Lieven|4=de|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Alexandra von Lieven|qq=|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work}} : ''Outline of the course of the stars – The so-called Nutbook'' . The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies (u. a.), Copenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5 . {{translated page|de|Zwölfte Tagesstunde (Altes Ägypten)|version=166733299|comment=Imported full de history}} [[de:Zwölfte Tagesstunde (Altes Ägypten)]] [[fr:User:Twelfth hour of the day (Ancient Egypt)]] {{wikidata link|Q245251}} [[Category:Ancient Egyptian astronomy]] [[Category:Egyptian calendar]] [[Category:History of timekeeping]] [[Category:Wikidata no English label]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]]

In ancient Egypt, the times of day were represented by the {{ill|Hour deities|lt=hour goddesses|de|Stundengottheiten|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hour deities|qq=|WD=Q2359142|1=Hour deities}} . From the explanations regarding the {{ill|Amduat|4=de|6=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amduat|qq=|WD=Q460509|1=Amduat}}, it can be seen that at the end of the twelfth hour of the day, when {{ill|Ra|en|Ra|WD=Q1252904|lt=Re|1=Ra}} begins to sink below the horizon, he is in the region of the {{ill|Qenqenet|4=de|6=pt|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Qenqenet|qq=|WD=Q1300614|1=Qenqenet}}, immediately in front of the “gateway to the {{ill|Duat|en|Duat|WD=Q871389|lt=Duat|1=Duat}} ”. == See also == * {{ill|First hour of the day (Ancient Egypt)|lt=1st Hour|de|Erste Tagesstunde (Altes Ägypten)|simple|User:Immanuelle/First hour of the day (Ancient Egypt)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/First hour of the day (Ancient Egypt)|qq=|WD=Q1362046|1=First hour of the day (Ancient Egypt)}} * {{ill|Book of the Day|de|Buch des Tages|en|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Book of the Day|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Book of the Day|qq=|WD=Q997784|1=Book of the Day}} == literature == * {{ill|Christian Leitz|fr|Christian Leitz|en|Christian Leitz|4=de|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Christian Leitz|qq=|WD=Q1080802|1=Christian Leitz}} : ''Ancient Egyptian star clocks'' . Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 9-0683-1669-9 . * Jürgen Osing: ''Hieratic papyri from Tebtunis I'' . Museum Tusculanum Press, Copenhagen 1998, ISBN 8-7728-9280-3 . * {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|fr|Alexandra von Lieven|4=de|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Alexandra von Lieven|qq=|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work}} : ''Outline of the course of the stars – The so-called Nutbook'' . The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies (u. a.), Copenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5 . {{translated page|de|Zwölfte Tagesstunde (Altes Ägypten)|version=166733299|comment=Imported full de history}} [[de:Zwölfte Tagesstunde (Altes Ägypten)]] [[fr:User:Twelfth hour of the day (Ancient Egypt)]] {{wikidata link|Q245251}} [[Category:Ancient Egyptian astronomy]] [[Category:Egyptian calendar]] [[Category:History of timekeeping]] [[Category:Wikidata no English label]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:Pages with unclosed parentheses]] [[Category:pages]]

''That hides the {{ill|Ra|en|Ra|WD=Q1252904|lt=shining one|1=Ra}}''}} In {{ill|Ancient Egypt|en|Ancient Egypt|WD=Q11768|lt=ancient Egypt,|1=Ancient Egypt}} the '''{{ill|Twelfth hour of the day (Ancient Egypt)|lt=12th Hour|de|Zwölfte Tagesstunde (Altes Ägypten|simple|User:Immanuelle/Twelfth hour of the day (Ancient Egypt)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Twelfth hour of the day (Ancient Egypt)|qq=|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work}}''' of the day (also '''twelfth hour of the day, "setting of Re in the west"''' ) was the twelfth hour of the day and was the "hour of {{ill|Sunset|en|Sunset|WD=Q166564|lt=Sunset|1=Sunset}} ", which ended with the beginning of {{ill|Twilight|en|Twilight|WD=Q164160|lt=evening twilight|1=Twilight}} ( {{ill|First hour of the night (Ancient Egypt)|lt=First night hour|de|Erste Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|simple|User:Immanuelle/First hour of the night (Ancient Egypt)|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/First hour of the night (Ancient Egypt)|qq=|WD=Q1361948|1=First hour of the night (Ancient Egypt)}} ).
